skill.name: wp-abilities-api
skill.description: Use when working with the WordPress Abilities API (wp_register_ability, wp_register_ability_category, /wp-json/wp-abilities/v1/*, @wordpress/abilities) including defining abilities, categories, meta, REST exposure, and permissions checks for clients.
skill.summary: Guide WordPress Abilities API registration, REST exposure, permissions, and client consumption.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found Markdown inline code, PHP examples, and API terminology rather than executable risky behavior. The two network findings are public WordPress documentation links. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, command execution, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Terms Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged backticked Markdown terms and PHP documentation examples as Ruby or shell execution. These lines document WordPress hooks, REST paths, package names, and PHP registration examples; they do not execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown prose or fenced PHP example code. No shell invocation, process API, or runtime script is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: API Documentation Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged several lines as weak cryptography, but the reviewed lines describe the WordPress Abilities API and human-readable fields. No MD5, SHA1, cipher use, hashing function, or encryption workflow appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain API description text and labels, not cryptographic operations. The finding appears to be a keyword collision rather than a real security issue.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Public Documentation Links Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The only URLs are WordPress developer documentation references. They are not fetched by code and do not transmit user data or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain Markdown references to official WordPress documentation. There is no network client, webhook, credential handling, or automated request path.

content.value_statement: WordPress Abilities API work requires correct hooks, stable identifiers, REST metadata, and permission checks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through registration, exposure, client use, and debugging.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use WordPress Abilities API features in plugins, themes, or core work.
Guides PHP category and ability registration with the correct lifecycle hooks.
Identifies required metadata for REST visibility, read-only behavior, and ability descriptions.
Supports debugging when abilities or categories do not appear in REST responses.
Points client work toward the @wordpress/abilities package for JavaScript consumption.
limitations: Targets WordPress 6.9 and later unless the project provides a separate Abilities API package.
Requires project context to choose plugin, theme, mu-plugin, or core placement.
Does not provide a complete application architecture for every WordPress project.
Requires a running WordPress environment to verify REST endpoints.

use_cases.title: Register Plugin Abilities
use_cases.description: Add namespaced abilities, categories, schemas, metadata, and permission checks to a WordPress plugin.

use_cases.title: Debug REST Visibility
use_cases.description: Find why an ability or category does not appear under the wp-abilities REST namespace.

use_cases.title: Connect Client Interfaces
use_cases.description: Plan JavaScript usage of available abilities through @wordpress/abilities and REST responses.

prompt_templates.title: Check API Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this WordPress task and tell me whether the Abilities API is appropriate. Include required version checks and project context questions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Registration
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan the Abilities API registration for this plugin feature. Include category ID, ability ID, metadata, schemas, and permission callback needs.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Missing Ability
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this WordPress project and explain why the ability is missing from the wp-abilities REST endpoint. Check hooks, metadata, IDs, and loading order.

prompt_templates.title: Implement End to End
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement an Abilities API feature from PHP registration through REST verification and JavaScript consumption. Add focused tests where the project supports them.

output_examples.input: I need a read-only ability that exposes site information to a client.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with category registration, a stable ability ID, read-only metadata, REST exposure, and a permission review.

output_examples.input: My ability is registered, but it does not appear in the REST response.
output: Check that category registration runs before ability registration.
Confirm the ability uses the Abilities API init hook.
Verify REST exposure metadata and the queried namespace.

output_examples.input: Connect a block editor feature to an existing ability.
output_examples.output: A client integration checklist covering package use, build dependency checks, REST visibility, and UI permission gating.

best_practices: Use stable namespaced IDs because changing ability IDs is a breaking API change.
Register categories before abilities with the documented Abilities API hooks.
Add permission callbacks for abilities that modify data or expose restricted information.
anti_patterns: Registering abilities outside the Abilities API init hook.
Exposing abilities in REST without reviewing permissions and data sensitivity.
Skipping input and output schemas when the ability accepts structured data.

faq.question: What WordPress versions does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets WordPress 6.9 and later, with notes for projects that use a separate Abilities API package.

faq.question: Can it generate production PHP code?
faq.answer: Yes, when project context is available. It should still be reviewed and tested in the target WordPress environment.

faq.question: Does it cover REST API exposure?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers the wp-abilities REST namespace and the metadata needed for client visibility.

faq.question: Does it cover JavaScript clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It points client-side work toward @wordpress/abilities and related build checks.

faq.question: Does every ability need a permission callback?
faq.answer: Abilities that modify data or expose sensitive information should include a permission callback.

faq.question: Can it diagnose missing abilities?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks hook order, file loading, category IDs, REST metadata, and namespace usage.
